ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Drumstick Braised Pork Rice; closest match is a generic braised pork rice dish concept. The dish includes an additional fried chicken drumstick component not captured in the FoodOn label.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with meat — braised pork and fried chicken drumstick composite
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for this specific Singaporean composite dish. A0CKP (rice-based dish) is used as the closest defensible anchor. The fried chicken drumstick component is an additional element not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific composite Singaporean dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice (~200 g), a portion of braised pork belly (~120 g), and one fried chicken drumstick (~80 g).

nutrient_source.title: Drumstick Braised Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Drumstick Braised Pork Rice (cr_id G00010395). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous braised pork and fried chicken composite (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) and analogous rice-based composite dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Drumstick Braised Pork Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white rice (Asian-style, GI ~72), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The braised pork and fried chicken drumstick components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 63.5) / 100 ≈ 45.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 25.19 × 4 = 100.76 g; adjusted for rice fraction ~63.5 g net carbohydrate from rice component.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce used in braising
hypertension.guidance: The reported 231 mg sodium per 100 g likely underestimates true sodium intake per full serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce, sauce on the side, or limit portion size, as a full 400 g serving may deliver 600–900 mg sodium from braising liquids alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base with significant carbohydrate load and added sugar in braising sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 72 and approximately 100 g of carbohydrate per full serving, this dish produces a high glycaemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting brown rice, reducing the rice portion, or requesting reduced sugar in the braising preparation to lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and deep-fried chicken drumstick, combined with high sodium from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from braised pork belly and deep-fried chicken skin, alongside high sodium from soy-based braising sauce, elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit portion size, remove chicken skin, and request reduced-sodium sauce preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid from pork (red meat) and high sodium from braising sauce worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork belly is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork with chicken or tofu, increasing vegetable portions, and requesting reduced-sodium sauce to minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Drumstick Braised Pork Rice is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker and food-court dish consisting of steamed white rice served with tender braised pork belly and a crispy deep-fried chicken drumstick. The pork belly is slow-braised in a soy-based sauce typically made from light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, five-spice powder, and sugar, producing a rich, savoury, and slightly sweet flavour profile. The chicken drumstick is separately deep-fried to achieve a crispy exterior before being plated alongside the braised pork and rice. The dish is a composite of two distinct protein preparations — braised and fried — making it calorie-dense and flavourful. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 231 mg sodium and 3.7 g dietary fibre. The high-GI white rice base (estimated GI ~72) combined with the sugar in the braising sauce creates a high glycaemic load per serving, making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The sodium content from soy-based braising liquids is likely substantially higher than the composite per-100g figure suggests. Clinically, this dish is best consumed in moderation, with portion control on rice and sauce, and is not suitable for low-sodium or low-GI dietary regimens without modification.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010395, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish is present in the HPB database with a reviewed provenance status. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA analogous data and documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.3 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for a braised pork and fried chicken composite, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry; this is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of pork belly and fried chicken, and may reflect a composite averaging artefact. The HPB-reported value is preserved as-is per source fidelity rules. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycaemic load per serving (~45.7) is calculated for a 400 g serving. 5. The dish is not halal due to the presence of pork.
